messages.content: You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Detail the dissemination plan, exploitation roadmap, IP strategy, and open access commitments.
Write the Dissemination, exploitation and communication section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

2.2 Measures to Maximise Impact – Dissemination, Exploitation, Communication    
2.2.1 Impact Strategy  
We will develop an integrated dissemination, exploitation, and communication (DEC) strategy (Figure 2.2a) designed to maximise impact and to be sufficiently flexible so as to effectively address the different target groups for the solutions we propose. Our impact strategy aims to maximise the legacy of our project and the concrete actions will be outlined in the Dissemination, Exploitation and Communication Master Plan DECMP (D 7.1). This strategy paper will target stakeholders of the proposed value chain, such as AD operators, microalgae producers, biorefinery technology manufacturers, sensor system developers, aviation fuel/SAF producers, aviation industry stakeholders, potential by-product users (e.g. farmers, fertiliser manufacturers), academic community, fuel certification authorities, investors, policy makers, journalists and media companies, and consumers (i.e. people who fly). We will elaborate the following key DEC goals:  
Goal 1: raising awareness and informing target groups of importance and potential of microalgae alternative fuels  
Goal 2: engaging in a dialogue with stakeholders along the algae value chain  
Goal 3: enabling knowledge transfer amongst us on specific designs, data sets, and methodologies;  
Goal 4: fostering exploitation opportunities for further research, policy and commercial adaptation.  
[HORIZON-CL5-2023-D3-02-08] [Development of microalgae fuel production and purification technologies for advanced aviation fuels]  
Dissemination and training strategy (Figure 2.2a): We will select (1) the most suited journals, topics and events to publish the scientific results and reach relevant audience. They will be presented in (2) selected Open-Access outlets and conferences, while fostering collaboration and engagement with relevant target groups. A mapping exercise will be carried out to identify key EU projects relevant to the area (Task 7.3), such as those funded by HORIZON-CL5-2023-D3-02-08, HORIZON-CL5-2022-D3-03-07, HORIZON-CL4-2021-RESILIENCE-01-16, and REFOLUTION, FLITE, and ALIGHT. We will make a proactive approach to connect with these projects to promote mutual project workshops and seminars, with the view of organising joint seminars/workshops to expand the network and reach of dissemination activities. In addition, students and young researchers will benefit from a Research Summer School in microalgae fuel dedicated lecture series at UCD, UCAM and KIT. Different stakeholders will also get the opportunity to engage with our project through regular demonstration workshops at the pilot-scale algae fuel production facility that will be built at BIAE during our project lifetime. The evaluation of these actions (3) will be done through the review of citations, impact factor of journals, altimetric and feedback forms.  
Exploitation strategy (Figure 2.2a): We will (4) identify Key Exploitable Results (KERs), target markets, potential users or clients and define the IP Management Plan in our Consortium Agreement. This forms the basis for developing potential (5) exploitation pathways and associated IPR strategy. By (6) monitoring the market, we will review the exploitation strategy and refine it throughout our project. We will also deliver a commercialisation model (D7.4) and policy white paper (D7.5) in order to harness our project results, facilitate commercialisation and legislative adoption by relevant stakeholders beyond our project lifetime.  
Communication strategy (Figure 2.2a): A multi-layer communication strategy will support and complement the dissemination and exploitation activities, and will include (7) identification of appropriate communication channels and formulation of key messages for the various target groups. This plan will then be implemented to produce (8) original content including journalistic articles, press releases, videos, infographics and social media posts. Monitoring tools (9) will measure the outreach of these actions and, while some actions may achieve a better outreach than expected (and can be repeated), other actions that are less successful can be adjusted.  
2.2.2 Dissemination + Training Actions  
Table 2.2a – Planned dissemination actions for our project  
Industry workshops and networking events, webinars, and seminars  
(Some will be organised as joint events with other EU projects in the area)  
Goals 1-4 M13 – M48  
Key motivation: run industry/end-user workshops, webinars, seminars to engage stakeholders. Some of these events will be jointly organised with EU projects in similar microalgae, fuel, aviation areas. Target audience: AD operators, algae producers, fuel/SAF producers, aviation sector, by-product users, sensor manufacturers, industry associations (e.g. EABA), investors, university students, air passengers. KPIs: 20+ events, reaching 800+ stakeholders  
Workshops at pilot-scale algae fuel facility on AD site (Kilshane, Ireland) Goals 1-4 M37 – M48  
Key motivation: run demonstration workshops to engage stakeholders. Target audience: AD operators, algae producers, fuel/SAF producers, aviation sector, by-product users, sensor manufacturers, fuel certification authorities, industry associations (e.g. EABA), policymakers, investors, university students. KPIs: 4 workshops, reaching 800+ stakeholders  
Scientific publications (open access) Goals 1,3,4 M13 – M48  
Key motivation: exchange scientific knowledge, provide technical details and open data  
Target audience: Academic community; Potential Journals (Impact Factor): Algal Research (IF = 5.276), Bioresource Technology (IF = 11.889), Chemical Engineering Journal (IF = 16.744), Frontiers in Energy Research (IF = 3.858). KPIs: >10+ peer-reviewed journal publications, reaching > 500 academics & leading to > 150 citations  
Conference presentations and fairs Goals 1-4 M13 – M48  
Key motivation: present scientific methodologies, results, and their impact; Target audience: AD operators, algae producers, fuel/SAF producers, aviation sector, academic community; Potential Conferences: AlgaEurope, European Algae Industry Summit (ACI); KPIs: >12 presentations and >2 conference workshops reaching > 500 experts  
Microalgae Fuel Summer Research School Goals 1-4 M25 – M48  
Key motivation: provide summer research internships in microalgae fuel production with dedicated lectures on the technologies and methodologies developed in our project; Target audience: University students; KPIs: 2-8 weeks combined research internship and lecture sessions for 5-15 students, to be jointly coordinated/implemented by UCD, UCAM and KIT.  
Technical Brochure Goals 1-4 M45– M48  
Figure 2.2a - Impact strategy of our project  
[HORIZON-CL5-2023-D3-02-08] [Development of microalgae fuel production and purification technologies for advanced aviation fuels]  
2.2.3 Exploitation Action and IP Management  
We will implement exploitation plans (WP7) to optimise the impact of our project’s results after project completion. Exploitation will focus on creating new academic and commercial opportunities for algae fuel technologies and delivering fact-based information on renewable fuel technologies for policymakers. Our project is set up in such a way that the future opportunities for the exploitation of our project’s results can be maximised, as the technology feasibility (demonstrated at TRL4/5) will be complemented with techno-economic and life-cycle analysis to assess scalability. The 11 Key Exploitable Results (KERs), comprised of novel methodologies and technologies, are listed in Table 2.2b. We will identify exploitation means for each KER (e.g. in terms of licensing, spin-outs, or direct use) with the long-term ambition to contribute to the route to TRL9.  
Table 2.2b – Our KERs and summary of IP strategies by each partner  
KER Owner Means of exploitation and/or protection  
KER1: Autotrophic growth strategy on digestate A4F  
Utilisation of KER1 for the development of a new service for A4F’s clients based on the design, build operation and transfer (DBOT) of microalgae cultivation units using digestate.  
KER2: Membrane system for CO2 delivery to microalgae culture UCD  
Secure IP protection & pursue commercialisation activities via Technology Transfer Office & NovaUCD (innovation hub with experience in supporting 360 start-ups-€760M turnover- from 2004-2019). KER2 will consolidate UCD’s growing knowledge basis in microalgae biotechnology to support formation of Irish microalgal spin-out specialising in wastewater treatment.  
KER3: In-line sensor for bacterial detection/enumeration IRIS  
Further develop, secure IP and commercialise the pioneering Multirange Spectroscopic Platform (‘Fusion’) for in-line monitoring of complex samples in Industry 4.0. KER3 will demonstrate the platform’s capacity for microalgae and open new market avenues.  
KER4: UV irradiation system for bacterial control UCD  
KER4 and KER5 will consolidate UCD’s knowledge basis in microalgae for wastewater treatment and biorefinery production of valuable products. Same exploitation pathways as KER2.  
KER5: Low-energy cell disruption based on autolysis  
KER6: Lipid extraction strategy to minimise solvent KIT  
Secure IP protection through technology transfer division responsible for commercialisation. Gained knowledge on solvent system and overall process design (KER6) will be made available to equipment manufacturers.

You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Detail the dissemination plan, exploitation roadmap, IP strategy, and open access commitments.
Write the Dissemination, exploitation and communication section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

2.2 Economic and/or societal benefits

Scale up potential to gain and create new European or global markets: Thanks to the market research and business planning work performed in conjunction with the Horizon Results Booster in the REMADYL H2020 project, we verified significant market potential in the PVC recycling market, which will be further strengthened by our ability to measure the concentrations of both lead plasticiser (RS) and stabiliser (LIBS) legacy additives through a hybrid approach. The global PVC recycling market was valued at approximately USD 3.3 billion in 2023 and is expected to grow to USD 4.9 billion by 2028, at a CAGR of 8.0%. This growth is driven by increasing regulatory pressures and demand for sustainable materials across industries like construction and packaging​ (MarketsandMarkets, GlobeNewswire). Within this global market, Europe represents a significant portion of PVC recycling efforts due to the region's strict environmental regulations and focus on sustainability. The EU, being at the forefront of environmental regulation, has major recycling markets for PVC, especially in Germany, France, and the UK, where companies like Deceuninck, Veolia, Vynova, INEOS & PreZero (LOIs) are leading players ​(MarketsandMarkets). The European PVC market demonstrates significant scale valued at USD 7.42 billion in 2023 and estimated to reach USD 9.75 billion by 2031 (Verified Market Research). In terms of volume, Europe’s PVC demand reached 6.08 MMT in 2024, with projections of 8.74 MMT by 2034 (Expert Market Research). Recycling is a critical component, with Europe’s total installed plastics recycling capacity at 13.2 million tonnes in 2023, with over 1.1 MMT dedicated to PVC alone.
In relation to the ability to gain or create new European or global markets, Visum LRS is positioned as a next-generation PAT for real-time chemical monitoring in industrial environments. It represents a category-defining innovation, whereby the optical fusion of LIBS and Raman make it possible to separately acquire information from two contiguous and interrelated holistic levels (atomic and molecular respectively), i.e. ability to govern all chemical behaviour in nature, in a unique, compact and affordable device. From an industrial implementation perspective, we will design two different implementation strategies to be able to cover the majority of industrial processing scenarios: static spot (for single and non-conveyed samples), and dynamic spot (for moving samples). Our modular sorting subsystem will also be engineered for adaptability and high-throughput compatibility, enabling easy retrofit to existing processing lines or integration into OEM designs. As such, from a technological and technical industrial implementation level, we open the ability to cost-effectively and reliably service complex monitoring challenges in additional plastics identification and sorting applications (beyond PVC recycling), in other online waste recycling applications (Table 1), as well as process monitoring and inspection in mining, pharmaceuticals, biotechnology, environmental monitoring, health, defence among others. From a market reach perspective, IRIS has its inhouse direct sales and out bound marketing team, and customer support. For growth we have has a credible international growth strategy through our international distribution network (currently with a coverage in 27 countries in Europe, Latin America, Asia, N. Africa). For strategic acceleration, we will pursue partnerships with OEMS (e.g. PICVISA), whose equipment integration capabilities offer a scalable pathway to rapidly reach large industrial clients. These OEM agreements form the basis of a highly repeatable, capital-efficient GTM strategy for growth across verticals.
Positive impacts for Europe: Taking our market entry application of plastics recycling, the Plastics Recyclers Europe outlines the urgent need to ‘enhance monitoring systems to ensure compliance with recycled content targets and that recycled plastics meet the high standards required for critical applications’. The Visum LRS is a technology enabler for monitoring and compliance with tougher EU legislative requirements. In the target PVC recycling use case, the innovative dual spectroscopic fusion analyser for on-line waste recycling will enable recyclers to remove harmful legacy additives from PVC recycling streams securing the competitiveness and viability of the value chain, and protecting human health and the environment. PVC waste containing legacy lead plasticisers and stabilisers can be redirected to alternatives processing (e.g. extraction of the lead, etc.). Our technology could also be used for screening the importation of PVC products to Europe to prevent non-compliant products entering the EU market, for EU citizen protection. This is a vital requirement according to the Plastic Recyclers Europe, whereby the implementation of rigorous controls of compliance and quality requires equal enforcement applied to both domestic and imported goods22. As such the Visum LRS could also be used by non-EU producers to quantify the composition of the PVC-based products to evidence compliance with EU market requirements, opening a global market. This strengthens both market surveillance and Europe’s capacity for enforcement of REACH and RoHS regulations, supporting health and safety objectives. Considering the impacts for resource efficiency, circularity and contributions to the EU’s Green Deal of ensuring safe and viable PVC recycling, the following business case from Deceuninck is compelling: PVC windows have a lifespan of 35 years and that they can be processed at least 8 times without any impact on performance, resulting in a total lifespan of ≥300 years. PVC recycling significantly reduces greenhouse gas emissions and conserves resources compared to virgin PVC production, with economic benefits including job creation and reduced raw material costs.
In terms of European jobs, the plastics recycling industry employs over 30,000 people in Europe (source: Plastics Recyclers Europe). The PVC industry (covering resin producers, converters, and machinery manufacturers) directly & indirectly supports >over 530,000 jobs across the value chain. By enabling compliance and enhancing the competitiveness of EU-based recyclers & OEMs, VISUM LRS will help safeguard existing employment & the creation of new jobs through scaling production, integration, maintenance, and services. Full commercialisation of VISUM LRS in the PVC sector alone could support ⁓500 new high-skilled & technical jobs across the value chain (manufacturing, installation, service, and data-driven monitoring).
Moreover, VISUM LRS contributes to EU strategic autonomy by reducing dependence on non-EU sensor & PAT technologies, enabling internal compliance with EU regulations through ‘Made in Europe’ technology and innovation (whereby we are at all times committed to prioritising EU component suppliers for EU sovereign strategy support). The impacts of our technology for strengthening EU plastics recycling value chains, for retaining critical IP within Europe, and for reinforces the EU photonics and AI ecosystem will all contribute to industrial competitiveness and resilience.

You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Detail the dissemination plan, exploitation roadmap, IP strategy, and open access commitments.
Write the Dissemination, exploitation and communication section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

2.2 Measures to maximise impact-Dissemination, exploitation & communication 

First version of the ‘Plan for the dissemination and exploitation including communication activities’: Our planned measures to maximise the impact of our project are outlined in this initial ‘Plan for the dissemination and exploitation, including communication activities’ (PDEC). We count on the leadership of EPIC (European Photonics Industry Consortium) for driving forward the Communication, Dissemination & Stakeholder Engagement strategy (WP5), who will also support TEMATYS in the development of the Business Cases for the technological results (WP4), which will be underpinned by a strong Intellectual Property strategy (WP4) under the expert guidance of ICP who have secured over successful 1000 patents over the years.
Key Exploitable Results and value proposition design: PhotonX delivers 5 Key Exploitable Results (KERs), namely: KER1 Mid-IR ICL/QCL inline analyser; KER2 Surface-Enhanced Raman Spectroscopy (SERS) device; KER3 InGaAs SPAD camera with on-chip processing; KER4 Chemical sensing arrays; KER5 PhotonX multimodal integration platform (hardware + data fusion + AI analytics). These KERs are supported by a series of Adoption Enablers: Validated demonstrators in industrial environments, that prove reliability, derisk adoption; Standardisation contributions to foster interoperability, facilitate market entry and build industry confidence in the PhotonX technologies; Business case, IPR and exploitation roadmap, to ensure partners have a route to market; Co-creation methodologies and upskilling, to support user acceptance and workforce readiness.
Together, these KERs and enablers are central to the PDEC. To maximise impact, the consortium will develop open-access training materials, contribute to relevant standards, and engage stakeholders through workshops, conferences, and targeted dissemination. This will be complemented by value proposition design, stakeholder-specific messages, demonstrations, exploitation strategies, business planning, and standardisation actions.
The consortium will systematically assess, document, and refine the KERs throughout the project, aligning them with the needs of target industries, SMEs, technology providers, end-users, and policymakers, and defining clear exploitation routes to maximise uptake and impact beyond the funding period.
COMMUNICATION PLAN & TOOLBOX: We have defined a strategic, holistic communication plan that will be tailored to project needs and updated as it progresses. It is underpinned by clear communication goals:
■ To engage and network with the photonics ecosystem in Europe, accelerating awareness, uptake, and standardisation of the PhotonX multimodal sensing solutions, strengthening the European supply chain and reinforcing open strategic autonomy in key enabling technologies, and fostering future collaboration with technology providers, research infrastructures, and industry clusters. EPIC (world leading photonics industry association, representing >800 companies) will ensure PhotonX is positioned & networked within the photonics ecosystem.
■ To inform key industrial end-user sectors about PhotonX and its benefits for industrial efficiency and sustainability- process industries (pharmaceuticals/bioprocessing, recycling, steelmakers, and other segments), as well as from the future wider application pool (food & agriculture, environmental monitoring, healthcare, critical infrastructure, among others) both directly and through their associations, clusters, and platforms (e.g., A.Spire, EFFRA, FoodDrinkEurope, Water Europe, among others). This engagement will be fostered through targeted workshops, sector-specific case studies and presence at relevant trade fairs, conferences, and stakeholder forums.
■ To outreach to standardisation organisations, contributing to the development of improved, science-based standards. UNE provides a credible pathway to connect with relevant standardisation bodies and ensure the PhotonX outputs are aligned with and feed into ongoing standardisation efforts.
■ To achieve high visibility and recognition within the scientific community, fostering credibility, attracting collaboration opportunities, and ensuring that the project’s findings contribute to advancing the state of the art in photonics, multimodal sensing, AI-driven data fusion, and their application to complex industrial and societal challenges. We will publish in peer-reviewed journals, present at leading conferences, and contribute to scientific dialogues on advanced photonics, multimodal sensing, hybrid AI for data fusion, and their deployment in sustainable, resilient, and human-centric industrial and societal applications.
■ To establish collaboration with existing (and upcoming) projects (e.g. RETINA, and new projects funded under this call topic) and develop synergies with other relevant European, national & regional initiatives, funding programmes and platforms, such as Digital Europe Programme (DEP).
■ To raise awareness among society at large of the critical role that advanced photonics, integrated with complementary sensing and AI play in enabling a greener, more competitive Europe. We will produce engaging video clips, booklets, infographics, and thought-leadership articles to present key messages in clear and accessible formats for diverse audiences.
We will design and build a Communication Toolbox to achieve our communication goals and objectives effectively:
■ Visual identity and brand: We have designed a logo during bid preparation; in the project implementation phase we will invest effort in building brand identity (brand book and brand values).
■ Project website.
■ Informative material: both printed and digital formats (banners, posters, flyers, Application Notes, etc.) & multimedia (introductory video presenting the innovative PhotonX concept, video clips, digital flyers) formats.
9 tradeshows/exhibitions (e.g., PIC International Conference, Laser World of Photonics (Munich), SPIE Photonics Europe, Hannover Messe, Vision Stuttgart, SENSOR+TEST (Nuremberg), ECOC–European Conference on Optical Communication, and Control, Automation & Vision Expo (Copenhagen), ACHEMA, CHPI Europe, etc.) face2face meetings, targeted mailings, and direct outreach to relevant stakeholders.
■ Commercial publications: ≥6 articles in online and offline trade magazines, blogs, and other professional outlets to reach industrial and technical audiences.
■ Public Relations strategy: Timely press releases aligned with key project milestones and outputs, distributed through appropriate press channels, including the European Commission News Section, as well as through industry platforms, networks, and associations (e.g. EPIC, Photonics21, SPIE, EFFRA, A.SPIRE, European Federation of Pharmaceutical Industries and Associations (EFPIA), European Steel Association (EUROFER), and relevant Horizon Europe thematic clusters).
Dissemination of the Project Results: Aligned with our Open Science Strategy, we will proactively share all non-confidential project outcomes widely and promptly through various means, including scientific publications in photonic sensor advancements, multimodal integration, sensor fusion and machine learning approaches sensor data analysis, algorithm integration for enhanced processing capabilities and decision-making accuracy of photonic sensors, and their application in sustainable, resilient, and human-centric monitoring across diverse industrial and societal domains. Our Dissemination strategy will: ■ Identify results for effective transfer of knowledge to relevant stakeholders; ■ identify suitable dissemination channels; ■ Optimise research impact, extending the value of our findings beyond the target applications and use cases of this project (pharmaceutical bioprocessing, steelmaking, and plastics recycling) into additional domains such as healthcare, security, infrastructure, transportation safety, and other societal areas where multimodal sensing and AI-driven decision support can enable sustainable and resilient outcomes; and assess the outcomes of dissemination activities. Key dissemination tools include, among others:
■ Open Access Scientific publications  prepared by partners submitted in relevant journals, e.g. Sensors, Optics Express, Journal of Photonics for Energy, IEEE Sensors Journal, Applied Spectroscopy, Nature Communications, Advanced Optical Materials, and Measurement Science and Technology.
20 international conferences- oral/poster/paper contributions, e.g., SPIE Photonics Europe, Laser World of Photonics Congress (Munich), CLEO/Europe-EQEC, IEEE Sensors, ECOC (European Conference on Optical Communication), OSA Frontiers in Optics, Optica Imaging and Applied Optics Congress, EUROSENSORS, and SENSOR+TEST, among others.
■ Organisation of Annual workshops/symposia (x3) on themes related to Multimodal Sensing and Intelligent Monitoring for Sustainable Systems (some will be online & some will be held within existing conferences)
■ Sharing non-confidential results on online media and data platforms (e.g., Zenodo, ResearchGate, GitHub, project website) including research data, software, reports.
■ Organisation of ≥3 training and workshop sessions, aligned with the validation phase of the PhotonX demonstration prototypes, to train end-users and stakeholders on the operation, configuration, and interpretation of the multimodal sensing platform. These sessions will also be made accessible online, contributing to the development of relevant skills and competencies across target sectors. Where appropriate, the training will leverage resources and networks of the European Institute of Innovation and Technology (EIT), Photonics21, and other relevant European initiatives to support future exploitation and wider adoption of the technology.
■ Final dissemination conference in Brussels (~M34) organised by EPIC in collaboration with relevant European platforms and networks (e.g., Photonics21….), to showcase the PhotonX results within the photonics ecosystem, as well as to industry end-user representatives, policymakers, and wider stakeholders.
Exploitation strategy and business cases: The key goal of our exploitation strategy is to maximise the use of the PhotonX KERs for commercial, societal, scientific, standardisation and policy-relevant purposes. The envisaged exploitation actions are outlined below, and each partner’s specific exploitation intentions are linked to their positioning on the exploitation value chain: Technology SMEs (ALPES- KER1, NV-KER3, IRIS- KER2 + KER5, JLM-KER4) drive technology push, develop photonic and non-optical modules, secure IP, and prepare for industrialisation. Industry early adopters (CELSA, Van Werven, MyBiotech) provide market pull, co-create use case demonstrators, validate in operational environments and prepare for adoption. Research (USAAR, UNE): Advance fundamental R&D, perform lab-scale validation, and ensure technology transfer; Innovation actors (EPIC, TEMA, BRITEST, UNE, ICP etc.): Facilitate standardisation, ecosystem engagement, dissemination, and access to finance/investors, and assist in securing IP assets.
■ Commercial and Industrial adoption of the KERs supported by Business Cases: TEMA (with support from EPIC) will work with the technology partners in defining the post project scale-up and commercial exploitation pathways for the results, both from a standalone perspective (where relevant), and as an integrated offering with Business Cases designed around each of the validated use cases (in pharmaceutical bioprocessing, steelmaking, plastics recycling)  for developing sector-specific offerings, supported by:
A clear value proposition per domain, quantifying demonstrated benefits, and analysing user and customer needs, assessing cost-effectiveness, and defining the expected market position and competitive advantage
A roadmap of the technical scale-up plan, Time-To-Market, Go-to-Market (GTM) and commercialisation strategies (e.g. starting with niche, high-margin markets before scaling to broader applications). It will also include an assessment of regulatory & market barriers, and strategies for EU supply chain consolidation.
Protection of key innovations through IP filings and knowledge encapsulation (guided by ICP-discussed below)
Financing strategies for securing the follow-on funding and investments needed to reach higher TRLs and full-scale EU deployment.
Active engagement with EU initiatives (EPIC, DEP, Photonics21) to enhance visibility and partnerships.
A periodic PESTLE analysis (building from Table 13) will inform these activities by continuously updating our understanding of the external conditions (political, economic, social, technological, legal, and environmental) crucial for success. Industrial partners’ post-project commitments, including supply chain alignment, product robustness, activating strategic distribution channels, and ensuring user acceptance, will also be formalised. By project end, PhotonX will have delivered a validated, multimodal sensing platform, supported by a robust exploitation roadmap, ensuring that the innovations developed contribute to EU sovereignty, competitiveness, and sustainability goals.
■ Knowledge and skills development: Use of the project’s knowledge & expertise to train workers and professionals with the skills required to safely and effectively use the PhotonX innovations (led by expert partner BRITEST), supporting broader uptake and deployment across the wider EU process industries, and follow on applications (e.g. environmental monitoring, healthcare, agriculture).
■ Intellectual Property and patenting: We target the publication of ≥3-5 patents arising from the project, as further detailed under the Strategy for IP Management.
■ Scientific and academic exploitation: The project results will also feed into further research activities, including scale-up studies, development of new research lines, and academic use in PhD and post-doctoral research. We also anticipate further scientific publications, both during and after the project, contributing to the state of the art in the field of multimodal sensing, advanced photonics and AI-enhancements.
■ Contribution to standardisation: Under the leadership of UNE, PhotonX will contribute to pre-standardisation documents and technical reports that support innovative industrial and societal applications of multimodal sensing. The project will maintain regular contact with relevant standardisation committees (e.g., CEN-CENELEC, ISO/TC 172 Optics and Photonics, IEC/TC 76 Optical Radiation Safety and Laser Equipment, and others), ensuring that feedback from these stakeholders is incorporated and that PhotonX’s findings contribute to the development of new or updated standards, in alignment with the EU Knowledge Valorisation Principles fostering open science, intellectual property management, standardisation, and societal engagement.
ategy for IP Management: The PhotonX consortium has identified strategically important and commercially sensitive innovations stemming from the project work with strong licencing and commercialisation potential. As set out in Task 4.3 IP Protection and Patenting, our strategy for knowledge management and protection is to file, within the lifetime of the project, 3-5 new patents, alongside exploring other IPR tools such as trademarks, copyright and Trade Secrets, where appropriate.
To ensure Freedom to Operate (FTO) and protect project results, initial patentability studies using EPO and WIPO patent libraries have been conducted. These studies and in-depth patent landscaping will continue throughout PhotonX to ensure FTO for project outcomes considered for IP application and patenting. A Technology Watch activity performed under Task 4.3 will regularly inform the consortium on technology developments and advancements in the research community, highlighting emerging IP, relevant publications and articles. A complete IP strategy and associated IPR portfolio will be generated and maintained under Task 4.3, led by ICP using its proven IPR methodology (Fig. 9). As a strategic partner, ICP brings extensive expertise, having supported over 1,000 successfully granted/issued patents that underpin commercial solutions, products, assets and services, and has developed an inhouse IPR protection framework tailored to multi-party EC-funded R&I projects such as PhotonX. This framework, successfully applied in numerous IAs and RIAs since 2017, has achieved a high grant/issuance success rate across patents filed, advancing the competitiveness of many EU SMEs. Our efforts will focus on protecting commercially sensitive innovations, while also fostering open science where appropriate. The consortium recognises the importance of balancing IPR protection with openness, to allow other EU researchers to build on PhotonX outcomes. This commitment is reflected in our clustering and ecosystem engagement activities (Task 5.3), our adherence to open science practices (Subtask 6.3.2), including open access conferences and publishing, as well as adopting FAIR data principles (Task 6.3.1).
Knowledge Management: The management of knowledge and IPR in the PhotonX project will be governed by the  Consortium Agreement (CA), which will implement Grant Agreement regulations on IPR protection, among other aspects such as funds distribution, decision procedures, risk management, legal aspects regarding software, trademarks, and patents, and the rights of each partner in the exploitation of results. Signed by all partners before project start, the CA will specify the Background knowledge made available for project use, ownership of which will remain with the current owners, and will be made available to project members royalty-free where necessary for their project activities. No Background IPR from Third Parties is expected to be needed. Foreground knowledge is owned by the partner(s) generating it. Where multiple partners contribute, ownership will be shared pro rata based on effort. Foreground knowledge will also be made available royalty-free to other partners if necessary for their project activities. Where possible, both background and foreground knowledge will be made available to other project partners for (i) dissemination, research, demonstration, interoperability of software and academic purposes, respecting the IPR of the generating partner; and (ii) exploitation of dependent results under fair and reasonable conditions. Requests for such access will respect normal commercial/security/IPR considerations and will require the granting partner’s approval. #§COM-DIS-VIS-CDV§#

You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Detail the dissemination plan, exploitation roadmap, IP strategy, and open access commitments.
Write the Dissemination, exploitation and communication section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

2.2 Measures to maximise impact-Dissemination, exploitation & communication 

First version of the ‘Plan for the dissemination and exploitation including communication activities: Our planned measures to maximise the impact of our project are outlined in this initial ‘Plan for the dissemination and exploitation, including communication activities’ (PDEC), which will be updated and implemented in WP7.
Key Exploitable Results and value proposition design: NextSteel’s systems-level approach will develop and integrate a set of Key Exploitable Results (KERs), shown in Table 10, which form the foundation of our PDEC. These will guide value propositions, communication, training, demonstration, and standardisation. The consortium will assess and refine KERs throughout the project, aligning them with stakeholder needs and defining clear exploitation routes (summarised in Table 7). Supported by pilot validations, LCA/LCC analyses, a strong business case, and input to standards, the KERs will be robust, investment-ready, and aligned with industrial, environmental, and societal goals.
Table 10- Key Exploitable Results, Target Groups and Exploitation Route
Communication Plan and Toolbox: We have defined a strategic and holistic communications approach built around the following clear communication goals and objectives:
■ To raise societal awareness of how NextSteel tackles the urgent challenge of decarbonising one of the most CO₂-intensive industries while preserving competitiveness, securing supply chains, and supporting a just transition. By enabling cleaner, more efficient steelmaking, the project contributes to climate goals, energy resilience, and sustainable growth. Key messages will be shared through engaging videos, infographics, articles, and other accessible formats.
■ To engage with industry (reached directly and via their Associations, Clusters, etc. such as Eurofer – European Steel Association, A. SPIRE, etc.) and to communicate NextSteel’s high industrial relevance in providing scalable, cost-effective pathways to decarbonise iron and steelmaking, enabling steelmakers to comply with tightening emissions regulations while maintaining product quality and operational efficiency.
■ To establish synergies with external bodies, such as standardisation organizations, contributing to improved science-based standards. UNE provides a very credible pathway to connect with relevant standardisation bodies and ensure that NextSteels’s outputs are aligned with and feed into ongoing standardisation efforts.
■ To achieve high visibility and recognition within the scientific community, fostering credibility, attracting collaboration opportunities, and ensuring that the project’s findings contribute to advancing the state of the art in sustainable steelmaking technologies and ≥8 joint activities with other relevant EU-funded projects.
We will design and build a Communication Toolbox for achieving our communication goals and objectives:
■Visual identity and brand: We have designed a logo during bid preparation, and in the project implementation phase we will invest effort in building brand identity (brand book and brand values).
■Project website and social media channels: The website will be a key channel for the project’s Communication (and dissemination) effort, hosting project public deliverables, blog content (e.g. >4 Thought Leadership articles, >2 whitepapers), ≥ 6 newsletters, and other content. The website will also serve as a hub for content shared via the established social media channels of the consortium partners (LinkedIn, X, Facebook, Instagram, among others), leveraging their existing loyal communities. We will complement this with dedicated NEXTSTEEL LinkedIn and YouTube channels and promote hashtags such as #NextSteel and #LowCarbonSteel to amplify reach.
■ Informative material: public materials in both printable and digital formats (banners, posters, flyers, Application Notes, Whitepapers, etc.) & multimedia formats (introductory video presenting the NextSteel concept, digital flyers).
■Engagement with industry: participation in ≥6 tradeshows/exhibitions (e.g. Green Steel World Expo & Conference), face2face meetings, targeted mailings, and direct outreach to relevant stakeholders.
■Commercial Publications: ≥ 6 articles in online/offline magazines, blogs, and other professional outlets to reach industrial and technical audiences.
■Public Relations (PR) strategy: Timely Press Releases aligned with key milestones and outputs, distributed       through appropriate press channels, including the European Commission News Section, and platforms, networks, and Associations, such as Clean Steel Partnership, A.Spire, Eurofer (European Steel Association), Green Steel World.
Dissemination of the Project Results: Aligned with our Open Science Strategy, we will proactively share all non-confidential outcomes through scientific publications and other channels. The dissemination strategy will: identify key results for effective transfer of knowledge to relevant stakeholders; identify suitable dissemination channels; maximise research impact by extending the value of our findings beyond the initial scope (i.e. additional uses of the process in other applications, as well as extending the use cases of the products in other steelmaking, non-ferrous or other process industries); continually assess outcomes of dissemination activities. Key dissemination tools include:
■Open Access (OA) Scientific publications (≥20) in relevant journals, e.g. Journal of Iron and Steel Research International, Steel Research International, Metals, Industrial & Engineering Chemistry Research, among others.
■Participation in ≥ 20 international conferences- oral/poster/papers, e.g., European Steel Forum (ESF), Global Steel Sustainability Summit, Nordic Steel, among others.
■Organisation of 4xAnnual symposia on CO2-neutral steel production (could be organised in existing conferences)
■Sharing non-confidential results on online media and data platforms (e.g. Zenodo, ResearchGate, project website, etc.) including research data, software, reports.
■Organisation of ≥4 training and workshop sessions, to coincide with the validation phase of the demo prototype training workers on the operation of the NextSteel pilots and on the use of the products in steelmaking. These sessions will also be accessible online, contributing to the development of skills and competencies to support exploitation.
■Final dissemination conference in Brussels (~M46) in collaboration with EU Partnership on Clean Steel to demonstrate results to potential users/customers and wider stakeholders.
Exploitation actions: Our exploitation strategy is to use the NextSteel KERs (see Table 10) for commercial/business, societal, scientific, contribution to standards and policy-relevant purposes. Envisaged exploitation actions are outlined below. Each partner’s exploitation intentions are linked to the KERs they (co)own (see Table 7):
■ Commercial/Industry adoption: NextSteel’s commercial/industry exploitation operates at two complementary levels: (i) system level, where a robust business case and exploitation strategy will be developed for at least one validated low-carbon steelmaking route, demonstrating technical, economic, and market viability, and (ii) component level, where individual KERs underpinning the route will have tailored exploitation strategies, enabling them to be deployed within the integrated route or as standalone assets. The route-level business case validates the combined value of the KERs, while the KER-level strategies provide flexibility and resilience in exploitation. A comprehensive NextSteel Business Case and Exploitation Strategy will be developed, identifying targeted markets (EU and global), analysing user needs, cost-effectiveness, and market positioning. A PESTLE analysis (Table 9) will guide regular updates on external factors, while post-project commitments of industrial partners will be formalised- including matching value chains, enhancing product robustness, securing industrial integrators, and user acceptance. This integrated approach (implemented via Task 6.5) ensures that NEXSTEEL route(s) and the KERs are aligned with market needs, industrial and commercialisation opportunities, supporting successful post-project uptake and scale-up.
■Training and skills development: Use of the project’s knowledge and expertise to train workers & professionals with new skillsets for the safe & correct use of NEXTSTEEL processes/technologies, supporting future uptake.
■Intellectual Property and patenting: Publication of ≥3 patents as discussed under 'Strategy for IP Management’.
■Scientific and academic exploitation: The project results will feed into further research activities, including scale up activities, development of new research lines, the transfer of the results to other Energy Intensive Industries, and academic use in post-doctoral research. We also anticipate further Scientific Publications in the post-project.
■Contribution to standards: Contribute to pre-standardisation documents and technical reports (target: ≥2) to support achieving innovative industrial applications of advanced clean steel grades. NEXTSTEEL will be linked with ongoing standardization work through regular contact with relevant committees in the field, e.g. ISO/TC 197 and CEN/CLC/JTC 6 on Hydrogen Technologies, CEN/TC 335 and ISO/TC 238 on Solid Biofuels, CEN/TC 308 and ISO/TC 275 on Sludge Recovery, etc. The inclusion of the produced bioanthracite as a thermally graded biofuel in the ISO 17225-8 will be evaluated. This will support the dissemination of the project and its results, getting feedback from the stakeholders organized in standardization committees and contribute to the development of new standards to support NextSteel exploitation according to the EU Knowledge Valorisation Principles.
ategy for IP Management: NextSteel will ensure protection and exploitation of commercially valuable innovations, strengthening IPR from EU funded R&I projects, as reflected in EC communications, annual IPR dashboards, IPR Enforcement Action Plan, Digital Single Market strategies & recently enacted tenders for boosting IPR and commercialisation from research results. Initial patentability studies using EPO & WIPO patent libraries have been conducted to confirm Freedom to Operate (FTO) and novelty. These studies and in-depth patent landscaping will continue throughout NextSteel to ensure FTO for project outcomes considered for IP application and patenting. Technology Watch will regularly inform the consortium on technology developments & advancements, highlighting emerging IP, relevant publications and articles. A complete IP strategy and associated IPR portfolio will be generated and maintained under Task 7.3 IP Protection and Patenting, led by ICP and using their proven IP assessment framework  (1,000+ successfully granted/issued patents from IAs and RIAs since 2017). The strategy is to file ≥ 3 new patents, alongside exploring other IPR tools (trademarks, copyright, Trade Secrets). E.g. REC foresees patent applications for chemical leaching for selected inorganics extraction from hydrochar and fertilizers production, & for catalytic pyrolysis followed by minerals washing & recovery for bioanthracite. Shared knowhow will also be generated by REC, CEA & POLI related to the integrated system for biowaste conversion into biogenic reducing agents. CEA will generate knowhow & patentable IP in relation to the catalytic SCWG. IPR protection of commercially sensitive innovations will be balanced with open science to allow other EU researchers to build on NextSteel outcomes, in line with Clean Steel Partnership. This commitment is reflected in our clustering & ecosystem engagement activities (T7.2), adherence to open science practices (ST7.1.2), including OA conferences and publishing, and adopting FAIR data principles (T5.5). Knowledge Management will follow the Consortium Agreement (CA), specifying ownership, access rights, and conditions for Background and Foreground knowledge. Access will be granted royalty-free where needed for project activities and on fair conditions for further use, ensuring secure and fair exploitation across partners. #§COM-DIS-VIS-CDV§#
